title.emphasis: Introduction: Building Contracts from First Principles

section.para: The success of a creative enterprise that cultivates artistic talent hinges on the integrity and clarity of its legal agreements. Rather than relying on monolithic, one-size-fits-all templates, a more resilient and adaptable system can be built from the ground up. This report outlines a constructive legal framework that deconstructs contracts into their essential components, allowing for the creation of precise, fit-for-purpose agreements that align with the evolving nature of the artist-patron relationship.
This model is built on two core concepts:

listitem.para: The Four Pillars of a Valid Contract: Every agreement, regardless of complexity, must satisfy four necessary and sufficient conditions to be legally valid: Mutual Assent, Capacity, Consideration, and a Lawful Object.1

The Two Planes of Contractual Terms: All legal terms can be divided into two categories: non-negotiable (mandatory) rules dictated by law, and negotiable (default) terms that parties are free to define for themselves.3

section.para: By using these principles as a blueprint, we can design a modular system where a Master Service Agreement (MSA) establishes the non-negotiable legal foundation, and a Statement of Work (SOW) defines the specific, negotiable terms of each creative project.
